name: Monopoly
description: The board game of property, rent, and bankruptcy — in play since the Great Depression.

questions.q: How many standard property spaces (deeds) are there on a U.S. Monopoly board?

source.notes: 22 colored properties + 4 railroads + 2 utilities.

questions.q: How many total spaces are on a standard Monopoly board?

source.notes: 10 per side of the square.

questions.q: How much money does each player start with in standard U.S. Monopoly?
In the U.S. standard edition, how much do you collect for passing GO?

source.notes: 'Collect $200 salary as you pass.'

questions.q: On the U.S. Monopoly board, what is the most expensive property (other than Boardwalk) in the dark-blue group?

source.notes: Park Place ($350) pairs with Boardwalk ($400).

questions.q: What three things happen when you 'Go to Jail'?
choices: Go to Jail, do not pass GO, do not collect $200
Lose your turn, pay $50, draw a card

source.notes: Triggered by the Go to Jail corner, Chance/Community Chest cards, or rolling three doubles.

questions.q: What are the two utilities in U.S. Monopoly?

source.notes: Purchased for $150 each.

questions.q: How much does each railroad cost in standard U.S. Monopoly?

source.notes: Rent doubles with each additional railroad owned (to $200 for all four).

questions.q: Which year did the game we know as Monopoly first hit shelves via Parker Brothers?

source.notes: Parker Brothers began mass production in 1935.

questions.q: Who is traditionally credited as the inventor of Monopoly, though an earlier designer's game underlies it?

source.notes: Elizabeth Magie's 'The Landlord's Game' (1904) is the precursor; Darrow popularized and patented his version.

questions.q: The U.S. Monopoly board's real-life property names come from which city's streets?

source.notes: Charles Darrow vacationed there; the properties match Atlantic City, NJ streets.

questions.q: On a classic board, what color group are Mediterranean Avenue and Baltic Avenue?

source.notes: The cheapest properties on the board.

questions.q: What color group is Boardwalk in U.S. Monopoly?

source.notes: Boardwalk ($400) is the most expensive property.

questions.q: What year did the UK version of Monopoly (using London streets) first appear?

source.notes: Created by Waddingtons, 1936.

questions.q: What's the rent on an unimproved Boardwalk when an opponent lands on it?

source.notes: Rent jumps dramatically with houses and a hotel.

questions.q: Which token was added to the U.S. Monopoly set in 2013 after a public vote?

source.notes: Replaced the iron.

questions.q: What do the question marks on the Chance squares signify to players?
choices: The card may help or hurt you
You must answer a trivia question
You cannot purchase property on this turn

source.notes: Draw a Chance card — random in effect.

questions.q: When you roll three doubles in a row, what happens?
choices: You draw a Community Chest card

source.notes: A rare but real rule many players forget.

questions.q: Who holds the UK-based rights to Monopoly today?

source.notes: Hasbro owns Monopoly globally since 1991.

questions.q: During WWII, a British intelligence service used a modified Monopoly set to do what?
choices: Hide maps, compasses, and real currency for smuggling to POWs

source.notes: MI9 slipped escape kits into Monopoly sets sent to German POW camps.

questions.q: Which Monopoly classic token was NOT an original 1935 piece?

source.notes: Race car was added in 1937.

questions.q: Which property group has the highest probability of being landed on during gameplay, statistically?

source.notes: Because Jail is a common landing spot, the three spaces 6-8-9 away (Orange: St. James, Tennessee, New York) are statistically frequent.
